title: Window replacement: when it pays and what to specify
subtitle: Double versus triple glazing, frame types, and BFRC ratings explained
excerpt: Replacing single-glazed windows with double glazing is worth doing. Replacing good double glazing with triple is rarely financially justified in the UK, though it makes sense in a near-Passivhaus build. The BFRC Window Energy Rating (WER) label shows both heat loss and solar gain in a single A-G rating.
sourceNotes: BFRC (British Fenestration Rating Council) Window Energy Rating methodology. gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B on window replacement U-value requirements. Energy Saving Trust window replacement guidance. GGF (Glass and Glazing Federation) technical publications.

content.text: of approximately 5.0-6.0 W/m2K. A standard double-glazed uPVC unit is 1.2-1.8 W/m2K. Replacing a 1.5 m2 single-glazed sash with a double-glazed unit saves roughly 14 kWh/year per m2 of glass at 10°C average temperature difference. A whole-house single-to-double replacement (30 m2 of glass) saves approximately 420 kWh/year, though the payback period on energy saving alone is typically 20-30 years.

The financial case is rarely the main reason to replace. Single-glazed windows cause cold surfaces, condensation, and discomfort in winter. Comfort and drier glass are usually what drive the decision; energy saving is secondary.

A triple-glazed unit has a U-value of approximately 0.6-0.8 W/m2K versus 1.0-1.4 W/m2K for a quality double. The improvement is real but the marginal saving over existing good double glazing is approximately 5-8 kWh/year/m2: a small difference at current UK energy prices. The payback on the extra cost of triple versus quality double is over 30 years.

Triple glazing makes sense in a new build aiming for near-Passivhaus performance, where windows are a large share of total heat loss. Fitting triple glazing into an existing house with average wall insulation is not justified on financial grounds.
What to specify when you do replace
Whole-window U-value: specify a maximum of 1.2 W/m2K. The Building Regulations minimum is 1.4 W/m2K; 1.2 W/m2K is achievable without significant cost premium.
BFRC Window Energy Rating: specify at least B. An A-rated window is worthwhile if you have south-facing glazing where solar gain is an asset.
: for south-facing glazing, a g-value of 0.6 or above admits useful solar gain in winter. For west-facing glazing in rooms prone to overheating, a lower g-value (0.4) reduces summer discomfort.

Frame material: uPVC, aluminium with a thermal break, and timber all reach similar U-values when specified well. uPVC costs least. Timber is repairable and has lower embodied carbon over a long life. Aluminium with a good thermal break is the most durable.

Gas fill: argon-filled cavities are standard. Krypton and xenon fills give marginal improvement at significant cost premium; only worth specifying if the cavity width is constrained (triple glazing in a slim frame).

ratings run from A++ (best) to G (worst). A rated means the window gains more energy from solar than it loses through conduction on a south-facing installation over a UK heating year. E or F rated means net loss. For replacement windows, B or A is the sensible specification.

Most window replacements in England are permitted development and need no planning permission, as long as the replacement looks similar to the original. Conservation areas and listed buildings need consent. In those cases, slim-profile double-glazed units and high-performance vacuum-glass products (such as Pilkington Spacia) are available. They cost more but meet conservation rules and cut the U-value from around 5.0 to about 1.8-2.0 W/m2K. Check with the local planning authority whether a specific glazing type is required.
